item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it's derivative of copyrighted material according to this article. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: Absolutely disgusting
item.content:encoded: So that's what happens when you put dead bodies on it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … so, is this 682's poop?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Here's the thing - if you reduce the surface area, like with the blowtorch, can't you just do that every time it expands?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would normally like this, apart from the whole "impossible to destroy part", as it implies that eventually it'll completely break containment and cover the Earth as there's no renewable way to contain it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Goddamn I love the ickiness getting worse and worse as the article goes. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, my hypocondria was a big part of what made this scary. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pun intended?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's already someone mentioning this in the forum, but I felt it was like "SCP 1742 meets SCP 505".
Got its own charisma though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I presume SCP-106 and this are never to come into contact with each other?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted, unfortunately. The writing it good, but it never impresses me enough to make it stand out from the various older articles back to which I can trace (it was probably unintentional) this one's defining features.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps they are related phenomena? You could put in a note lampshading this even.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, you're right…

SCP-1742 is a contagious phenomenon which takes the form of household filth, appearing as stains, marks, or a greasy coating on inanimate objects

Analysis of samples reveals a widely variable composition, including substances such as soil, complex lipids (kitchen grease), and in some cases, particles of human and domestic animal feces.

Objects coming in contact with an instance of SCP-1742-1 will develop a mark or stain at the point of contact

Attempts to remove SCP-1742 from an object via normal cleaning methods invariably spread the contamination instead

All ten units in the complex were destroyed in a controlled burn

I've never even heard of that article, and yet it looks like I plagiarised most of it.

That's… really depressing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is basically a keter version of SCP-1742.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think we need a tale now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Headcanon accepted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not filth. It's Toth. The Tothbrush exists to remove Toth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno, I don't find it particularly interesting. Until it starts taking people over, it just seems like urban decay. Plus, you tell us how it manifests twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With you on the potentially interesting test logs thing, but incineration might really be the best answer for a couple reasons:

Organic just means "molecules containing carbon atoms", so there might not really be anything to kill here. There's a difference between "dissolved" and "molecule no longer intact" as well: Most organic molecules at most separate into predictable ionic pieces that will reconnect voltron style when precipitated out of solution, and there are many like the petrolium family that just dissolve with molecules intact. There's also a measure of cross-testing type risk here; some normal organic compounds are otherwise stable and mostly harmless until you put them into solution, at which point they become highly toxic or explode violently at the slightest impact.

The foundation probably uses plasma arc incinerators for just this sort of reason: they literally vaporize things down to a hot plasma soup of component atoms and electrons at a temperature several times hotter than the surface of the sun. In the real world they use them for disposal of medical waste and toxic industrial byproducts because it's the only way to be completely sure you've annihilated all molecules of something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …and now that thing is headcanoned as possibly created to combat this stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This sounds like a job for The World's Best Tothbrush

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it, but it feels like it's missing something. A lot of the information given at the end was stuff we already knew, so it feels kinda redundant. I'm not saying it needs to have a super complex twist but something more than just "It's nasty and we can kill it with fire" would have been nice. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks; fixed it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unless he's using a weapon with an internal magazine. That's unlikely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it. However, small nitpick, it's 'magazine' not 'clip'.

item.title: Update
item.content:encoded: I've made some edits and added an experiment log which should answer some of your questions. Enjoy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. That was actually pretty scary.
+1

But I have to ask, why not just cut off the part of your skin infected with the stain?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dissolving an organic substance in something might kill it. I was thinking along the lines of an alcohol of something akin to that, and mostly because the SCiP was mentioned to be part organic. The soap was just because the SCiP is basically a grime incarnate (worth a try).

The concrete thing was just a curiosity. Sealing it in anything would be interesting: plastic, rubber, Plexiglas, molten steel. All of those would make interesting testing log entries in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Technically soaps and solvents don't destroy substances, just dissolve them, so there's part of your answer: those would make it worse by contaminating the solution itself with SCP-2194. Same thing for the concrete, probably.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I give it a +1 for now for its cool concept and clear writing, but I am curious about why/how it can't be destroyed. The article would suggest that this SCiP is capable of standing up to incineration, acid emersion(and base emersion too for that matter), any number of chemical solvents, and anything else the Foundation could throw at it. I'd say that only acknowledgement of the SCiP's indestructibility is wasted potential. You could write so much about how it just keeps spreading no matter what the Foundation dose, and that could expand to how the SCiP propagates. I would love to hear a technical breakdown of its capacities. You could even write that a Site was condemned because the Foundation brought it to one, and consequently, in their attempts to destroy it, couldn't stop it from spreading at all.

Edit:As an after thought, what if you just buried the SCiP under like a meter of concrete?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks - I hate it when I miss those.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fixed some remaining "SCP-XXXX"s and upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to say, I really like this. No gimmicky stuff, no big twists, nothing too weird. Just some nice, cool, creepy s**t.

+1
